decision.reason: Focused ship-loss candidate and promotion precheck were strong, but the measured runtime trial lowered the audio category score and did not resolve the full-theme active body/tail segmentation gap.

problem: Ship-loss audio is now the highest measured audio gap. The body/tail reference sub-events are not separated cleanly in the runtime active window, so the player death moment still reads as a collapsed high-energy segment under full-theme analysis.
strategy: Use the focused ship-loss candidate loop and promotion precheck as proposal gates, then accept a runtime change only after a real full-theme audio comparison, event-gap rollup, cue-alignment guardrail, runtime recovery guardrail, and quality score.

precheckWins: Theme average worst-segment risk improves by 0.27/10.

learning: Focused candidate scoring and promotion precheck are necessary but not sufficient for composite cues.
PlayerHit needs a contract-aware active-window strategy that can represent onset/body/tail separately in runtime or in scoring, not only a shorter clip window.
Runtime trial acceptance must compare the real post-edit audio theme, event-gap, alignment, recovery, and quality artifacts before promotion.
nextStep: Build a contract-aware playerHit candidate/pass that either separates death onset/body/tail into runtime cue phases or improves the scoring/capture strategy so the active body is evaluated against the intended sub-event.
